Therefore, the current pullback in the sector provides a great buying opportunity for patient investors as it increases the yield and the potential capital appreciation that can be obtained from these infrastructure investments over the long term.

Given the fact that the A.I. build-out and expected A.I. boom are driving strong energy demand in the United States, as well as expected strong demand for energy exports from the United States, the economic value of strategically located natural gas and NGL infrastructure appears to be quite high. Yet, the market has not seemed to want to price that in for some natural gas and NGL infrastructure players like OKE, ET, and EPD. As a result, investors have a great opportunity to take advantage of one of the best high-yield investing opportunities I have ever seen by loading up on these names. As a result, these, along with other midstream names, make up my largest position in my portfolio by far, including overweight positions in energy infrastructure in my Core and Retirement portfolios at High Yield Investor.

data.title: Disney: The Market Is Finally Waking Up To The Danger Of The YouTube TV Blackout
data.description:
After The Walt Disney Company (DIS) reported its full-year results for Fiscal Year 2025 yesterday, I at first expected the stock to trade choppily in a more or less sideways manner. What followed was instead a nearly 10% decline in the price of the stock, all the way down from $116 to $107. Analysts reactions, perhaps in part informed by the stock’s movements, have generally accentuated the negatives of the report.

What was interesting about this decline was that it wasn’t purely a reaction to the earnings report itself; the decline began in earnest after the subsequent conference call began. I believe management has made the wrong choice in terms of how it has chosen to frame and approach the ongoing blackout dispute with YouTube TV. It is this, not the earnings numbers themselves - which really weren’t that bad - that has sent the stock spiraling.

Be warned, we have several issues to cover, and I intend to go in-depth; I’m afraid this article will be a little longer than you’re used to. I hope you find it worth the extra few minutes.

The Report Itself Was Actually Solid

Before turning to the main issue, first, the report itself. It’s true that Disney did disclose a substantial decline in free cash flow in the fourth quarter, but I would argue it is a mistake to emphasize too much a number so inherently volatile on a quarter-to-quarter basis. Disney’s Parks, cruise liners, and other major CAPEX outlays can cause timing issues in FCF; the more stable annual FCF number was actually up 18% Y/Y.

Mind you, I wouldn’t put too much stock in that either; Disney also disclosed a number of one-off factors, including some tax allocation adjustments. I am not a tax lawyer and will not pretend to understand more than I do, but it seems reasonably clear from Disney’s own disclosures that the federal emergency declaration pertaining to the California wildfires has allowed the company to defer roughly $1.7 billion of taxes owed from this fiscal year to next. Strip that out, and FCF was more or less steady Y/Y. We could dive even deeper, but I would put that last number as far closer to the ‘true’ FCF than either of the previous two.

At the same time, Trump’s One Big Beautiful Bill Act will also offer a permanent reduction in taxes owed of some size, owing to the expansion of full expensing - the rare tax topic I do know something about - to more qualified spending. I doubt that will fully attenuate the impact of the $1.7 billion on next year’s numbers, but it will help. Until we see how much it will help, however, I am reluctant to base a recommendation on such an unknown.

ESPN's Ongoing, Hidden Decline

Let’s move on, then, to the company’s actual operations. The report was somewhat occluded by the fact that most of the most interesting things happening at Disney didn’t start until after the report ended; ESPN didn’t terminate its betting partnership with PENN Entertainment (PENN) and launch its new one with DraftKings (DKNG) until this month. ESPN Unlimited did launch in August, but Disney offered no numbers on it.

In fact, although it was not remarked upon by the analysts on the call, that was, to me, one of the more alarming elements of the report. To be sure, management insists that ESPN Unlimited is off to a fantastic start; they’re “thrilled” with the response.

But the company is offering less transparency into sports streaming instead of more. For a long time, I had to approach my ESPN analysis indirectly, even abstractly, because Disney did not break out ESPN’s performance separately from ABC, Disney Channel, and the rest. That finally changed; in late 2023, Disney broke out ESPN’s financials separately for the first time. Employees, and more importantly, former employees, were stunned to discover that the division was still reporting multibillion-dollar profits. The backlash was actually considerable, as employees questioned why such drastic layoffs were needed if the division was doing so well.

For the record, they shouldn’t have been quite so upset as all that; ESPN is not doing nearly as well as is portrayed. As I’ve explained before, much of the ESPN “profit” is in fact reallocated revenues from the Disney+ streaming service, funneled through ESPN+. In FY2024, almost 70% of ‘Sports’ profits were in fact ESPN+ profits, and almost all of those were really Disney+/Hulu profits. ‘Core’ ESPN is almost certainly now generating less than $1 billion in operating income per year. My previous article covered this in depth, so I won’t repeat those points again, you can see all my calculations demonstrating this there.

These trends have continued in the past fiscal year. In Disney’s Q2 earnings report in March, "Sports" reported $428 million in subscription fees. No, that’s not a typo; ESPN linear generates far more than that, of course, but linear monthly fees are called "affiliate fees." The term "subscription fees" connotes payments to the ESPN+ streaming service. Those payments, in turn, are largely payments on the Disney Bundle, which are allocated internally to ESPN+ by Disney, not by arm's-length transactions.

That $428 million represented 63% of the $687 million operating income of the division. That is up from the 54% they represented the prior year. Not surprising, considering that ESPN+ subscription fees are (nominally, by Disney’s internal accounting) rising while operating income at the Sports division is falling.

The Q3 report was broadly similar. Thus, ESPN Core’s operating income is falling even faster, driven by two things. One of them, of course, is cord-cutting.

Ever-Foggier Reporting

With the launch of ESPN Unlimited, however, Disney has closed this rare peek behind the curtain of its operations. In the just released report, Disney reported only a combined "affiliate and subscription fees" category for its Sports division; this despite the fact that it continues to report the two categories separately in its Entertainment division.

I won’t speculate as to reasons or motives; the fact is that all we know for sure is that transparency has been reduced. However, this does make it somewhat harder to determine how the interaction between streaming and linear is playing out in Disney’s operations. And I cannot help but think that if there were good news to report here, Disney would not be so reluctant to continue reporting it.

The issue is not unique to Disney, of course; I have said in the past that the line between streaming and linear has become so blurred at most legacy media companies that it is impossible to tell if the operating incomes being reported for each category fairly reflect the economics of each. The only thing you can really do is look at the company’s operations as a whole.

Earnings Essentials

So, I’m going to take my own advice. Overall, Disney reported revenue growth of 3% - basically at par with U.S. inflation - but held cost growth to 1%. This year’s net income of $12.4 billion was a massive improvement on last year’s $5 billion, but as you’d expect, there were one-offs there; in addition to the factors already discussed, Disney also netted $2.8 billion in tax benefits from the acquisition of Hulu and did not repeat last year’s $3.5 billion restructuring and impairment charges related to Star India and domestic linear networks.

Experiences operating income was up while Sports operating income was down domestically; the sale of Star India ended the red ink in Disney’s international Sports operations.

Altogether, after one-offs, net income was very similar in both years, averaging about $8.7 billion. At first glance, that’s actually not so bad; at a market average 24 P/E multiple, that would pencil out almost exactly to the $116 per share it went into the report with. After yesterday’s decline, its TTM P/E is only 22.1.

Blackout Becomes A Knockout

Unfortunately, after the report comes the conference call, and it is here that I believe things started to go off the rails. Disney has been enmeshed for the last two weeks in a blackout dispute - yes, yet another one - this time with Google’s (GOOG, GOOGL) YouTube TV. The dispute has defied expectations that it would be resolved quickly; in fact, having passed Wednesday with no deal, it is now officially the longest blackout dispute in Disney’s history.

This is a blackout involving Disney’s core linear TV platform and one of the largest pay-TV operators in the U.S.; up until yesterday, the market had simply not treated it with the requisite intensity of focus. In fact, Disney’s stock was actually up 5% since the blackout began going into earnings, a frankly ludicrous appreciation that apparently signified the market’s confidence that even the mighty YouTube would soon come crawling back to ESPN, which management has always insisted is essential to the survival of pay-TV operators.

It hasn’t worked out like that. YouTube has held out. Even the broadcast of Monday Night Football’s most prominent game of the year, the Eagles-Packers matchup on Monday, did not break the impasse. As the early optimism has become harder to maintain, CEO Bob Iger has increasingly confined his media appearances to friendly platforms, usually ones he controls; and as expected, the national blackout has been the most obtrusive issue not raised by the interviewers. With the earnings call, there was no ducking it any longer.

To be clear, management knew this was coming; that’s why they reportedly kicked negotiations with YouTube - presumably already a high priority - into another gear, which saw Iger himself reach out to Google’s senior management. Reports were that substantial progress had been made; indeed, some even said Disney made more progress in 12 hours than it made in the two weeks before that. I would put that down to hyperbole, but the truth is that even such a marker probably doesn’t set the bar very high.

Despite the progress, full resolution eluded the parties; when the conference call rolled around, Disney was still blacked out. Iger and CFO Hugh Johnson got their questions, but the answers were not what the market hoped.

Pushing All The Chips In

In a nutshell, Disney isn’t compromising; it’s doubling down. Iger insists that Disney will only return when it gets “fair value” for its content, and Johnson flatly warned that the already record-long dispute could go on “for a little while.” He also insisted in a subsequent interview that “we’re ready to go for as long as they are,” implying that Disney was financially able to absorb whatever losses the dispute may produce.

From the perspective of reassuring the market, this was about the worst thing they could do. For many reasons, but at the end of the day, I’m just not sure Johnson’s bravado about being able to absorb blackout losses is supported by the numbers, and it looks like the market is beginning to agree with me. Even other publications have noted that Disney’s losses are around $4 million per day.

That estimate was meant to be a somber reflection of Disney’s high stakes in this battle, but I must say, that calculation seems to me to understate the damage that a permanent blackout of YouTube TV would do. That estimate implies $120 million per month, which, with YouTube TV at an estimated 10 million subscribers, pencils out to $12 per month, per subscriber.

The True Damage Of The Blackout

That seems impossibly low. In the first place, Disney is losing advertising impressions as well as subscription payments when it is blacked out. True, advertising contracts often preclude actual refunds; the standard structure of an ad contract with a major broadcaster like Disney is to set a contractually guaranteed minimum impressions level, which if not met results in “make-goods,” or free ad slots the next quarter or year to bring total impressions up to the guaranteed level.

So, even if we assume that Disney is not losing any advertising cash flows this year, the blackout will still make itself felt in subsequent quarters cash flows. Which means it is losing advertising income right now. Still, for now we’ll ignore this and assume the $12 refers solely to affiliate revenues.

This past year Disney reported $5.7 billion in Entertainment Linear Networks affiliate fees and an imputed $9.1 billion in Sports Linear fees after deducting ESPN+ subsidies. With Disney reporting its core linear networks, including ESPN and Disney Channel, have 61 million subscribers, that pencils out to $20.35 per month in fees, not $12. This number aligns much better with the $20 credits YouTube TV has issued subscribers for the blackout inconvenience; it is highly unlikely YouTube would overpay subscribers for the savings not paying ESPN is generating.

If YouTube TV were to permanently black out Disney’s networks, its affiliate revenue loss alone would come to almost $2.5 billion. Including advertising revenue, the total loss would come to $3.5 billion - at a time when the total operating income of the affiliate networks at the Sports and Entertainment divisions combined comes to only $3.4 billion.

Projecting An Endgame

My base scenario is still that this gets resolved; frankly, Disney cannot afford not to resolve it. The question is what such a resolution will cost. Almost certainly, what is driving Disney’s stance here is not ignorance of the rock, but awareness of the hard place.

As I’ve written about before, all of Disney’s deals with top distributors contain Most-Favored-Nation clauses; these are basically guarantees that if Disney offers improved economic terms to any of DIRECTV, Comcast (CMCSA) or Charter (CHTR) it will offer it to all of them. YouTube TV has already been offered a similar provision, so there is now basically a new Big Four in pay TV that is essentially a negotiating composite; whenever Disney bargains with one of them, it is in fact bargaining with all of them, since they all will receive any agreed concessions.

How Far Does The Rubble Fly?

The above calculations suggest that Disney’s rational concessions are capped at $3.4 billion for all four operators combined; that means that Disney can offer a maximum of $850 million in concessions to each one, assuming that they are all similar sizes over the lifetime of its new YouTube TV deal. Charter and Comcast are larger now, but YouTube TV is the only one still growing.

The concessions would all be offered on the affiliate fee side; that pencils out to about $7 per subscriber per month in reductions for the Big Four at the very, very most; obviously Disney would like to offer less than that. It might get away with less; YouTube TV is presumably suffering at least a little too, and it might well settle for less than a 35% reduction in its content costs.

As I said, it gets resolved; the question is the extent of the damage. If the full $3.4 billion comes out of Disney’s operating income, the post-tax hit comes to about $2.5 billion; that would knock its fair value price all the way down to $84. If the damage is only half, it’s $95.

Investment Summary

I believe the market is reacting to the growing perception that Disney has overplayed its hand with YouTube, not the aberration of what are almost certainly transitory declines in free cash flow. And that reaction is not unjustified; after treating the blackout dispute with unwarranted indifference, the market is now waking up to just how much of a threat an ESPN-free YouTube TV is.

While the decline is not warranted by the timing issues of Disney’s earnings, the risk to Disney is real, and I still rate it avoid even after the decline.
